coredata.dc:title: Sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots sensors for nitric oxide fluorescence quantification
coredata.dc:creator: Simões, Eliana F.C.
Leitão, João M.M.
Esteves da Silva, Joaquim C.G.
coredata.dc:description: Microwave synthetized sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots responded selectively to nitric oxide (NO) at pH 7. Citric acid, urea and sodium thiosulfate in the proportion of 1:1:3 were used respectively as carbon, nitrogen and sulfur sources in the carbon dots microwave synthesis. For this synthesis, the three compounds were diluted in 15 mL of water and exposed for 5 min to a microwave radiation of 700 W. It is observed that the main factor contributing to the increased sensitivity and selectivity response to NO at pH 7 is the sodium thiosulfate used as sulfur source. A linear response range from 1 to 25 μM with a sensitivity of 16 μM−1 and a detection limit of 0.3 μM were obtained. The NO quantification capability was assessed in standard and in fortified serum solutions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Citric acid
Urea
Sodium thiosulfate
Nitric oxide
Fluorescence quenching
pH 7

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundação para a Ciência e a Tecnologia

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: A PhD grant SFRH/BD/81074/2011 to Eliana Simões and financial support, project PTDC/QEQQAN/5955/2014, from Fundação para a Ciência e a Tecnologia (FCT, Lisbon) are acknowledged. The use of FTIR from UCQFarma, Pharmacy Faculty of the University of Coimbra and the TEM analyses from Nanomaterials and Micromanufacturing group of Centre for Mechanical Engineering of the University of Coimbra (CEMUC) are also acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
NO, ONOO−, ClO−, HO, H2O2 and O2
− fluorescence response evaluation a) and NO time profile b) at 10, 50 and 100 μM concentrations.
FTIR spectra of the synthetized CDs with and without ST.
TEM images of the synthetized CDs.
Fluorescence time profiles and quenching effect found by addition of NO concentrations from 1 to 200 μM.
Fluorescence quenching effect obtained by addition of ROS/RNS or ionic species.
NO quantification results in standard solutions and in spiked serum samples found in the linear range from 1 to 25 μM.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para:
* a - Intercept; s
a - Intercept standard deviation; b - Slope; s
b - Slope standard deviation; s
y/x - Standard deviation of residuals; r - Linear correlation coefficient; m - Number of calibration points; LR – Linear range; LD – Limit of detection evaluated through the s of five determinations of a blank.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of NO detection by the synthetized CDs with the indicated by other CDs used in previous literature reports. a

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para:
Prec. – Synthesis precursors; Synt. – Method of synthesis; Funct. - Functionalization; Φ – Quantum yield; λ
EX – Excitation wavelength at maximum fluorescence intensity λ
EM – Emission wavelength at maximum fluorescence intensity; LR - Linear range; LOD – Limit of detection; n.p. – not presented; CA – Citric acid; EDA – 1,2-ethylenediamine; MW – Microwave synthesis; NPH - naphthalimide with an o-phenylenediamine group; UR – Urea; ST – Sodium thiosulfate.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para:
In these refs. the ionic species were not evaluated. In ref. ONOO−, H2O2, O2
−, HO, NO3 − and NO2 − and in ref. H2O2, HClO, 1O2, HO, NO3 −, NO2 − and ascorbic acid were the evaluated compounds.

head.ce:title: Sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots sensors for nitric oxide fluorescence quantification

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centro de Investigação em Química da Universidade do Porto (CIQ-UP), Faculdade de Farmácia da Universidade de Coimbra, Pólo das Ciências da Saúde, 3000-548 Coimbra, Portugal

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centro de Investigação em Química da Universidade do Porto (CIQ-UP)
Faculdade de Farmácia da Universidade de Coimbra

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centro de Investigação em Química da Universidade do Porto (CIQ-UP), Departamento de Geociências, Ambiente e Ordenamento do Território, Faculdade de Ciências da Universidade do Porto, R. Campo Alegre 687, 4169-007 Porto, Portugal

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centro de Investigação em Química da Universidade do Porto (CIQ-UP)
Faculdade de Ciências da Universidade do Porto

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Microwave synthetized sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots responded selectively to nitric oxide (NO) at pH 7. Citric acid, urea and sodium thiosulfate in the proportion of 1:1:3 were used respectively as carbon, nitrogen and sulfur sources in the carbon dots microwave synthesis. For this synthesis, the three compounds were diluted in 15 mL of water and exposed for 5 min to a microwave radiation of 700 W. It is observed that the main factor contributing to the increased sensitivity and selectivity response to NO at pH 7 is the sodium thiosulfate used as sulfur source. A linear response range from 1 to 25 μM with a sensitivity of 16 μM−1 and a detection limit of 0.3 μM were obtained. The NO quantification capability was assessed in standard and in fortified serum solutions.

ce:section.ce:para: Reactive Oxygen (ROS) and Nitrogen Species (RNS) are typically involved in numerous physiological and pathological processes acting as signaling molecules [1]. The nitric oxide (NO), one of most studied ROS/RNS, it is a free radical that plays as a physiological messenger and as effector in mammalian cells. It has a vital role in vascular and neuronal signal transduction, bioenergetics, immunity and cell death regulation. However at high concentrations it can be injurious to cells and can be associated with some diseases [2–5].

Sensors based in nanoparticles (NPs) have attracted much attention due their own specific characteristics [6]. Recently some sensors based in NPs have been developed for the NO detection [7–24]. NO optical nanosensors based in NPs as gold NPs (AuNPs), carbon nanotubes (CNT) and quantum dots (QDs) were developed. Two AuNPs optical sensors to NO detection based on immobilization of AuNPs onto a fiber optic were reported [7,8]. CNT were used in optical sensors with potential for NO sensing [9,10]. The NO detection using QDs is usually based on fluorescence quenching of QDs by NO. TEA modified CdSe-QDs were quenched with NO [11]. QDs with chitosan (CS) were used as NO sensors [12,13]. A CdSe-QDs sensor stabilized by CS and mercaptosuccinic acid was showed by Simões et al. [14]. A sensor synthetized by adsorption of CdSe/ZnS-QDs on polymethacrylate was presented [15]. An optical fiber sensor based on CdSe-QDs using cellulose as NO sensitive membrane was developed [16]. CdSe functionalized with trivalent porphirin were used in NO sensing [17]. Modified hyperbranched polyether nanospheres and QDs were reported in NO detection [18]. The detection based on the degradation of the polymethyl methacrylate molecule (PMMA) by NO in CdS-PMMA nanocomposites was presented [19]. Ferric dithiocarbamate compounds were used in the QDs functionalization for NO sensors [20,21]. Organogel–CdSe/ZnS QDs hybrid materials showed fluorescence sensitivity towards NO [22]. CdSe/ZnS-QDs coordinated with dirhodium complex were presented [23]. A QDs system capable of releasing NO and meanwhile detecting the NO release under conditions similar to physiological environment based on the fluorescence quenching by NO was presented [24].

The carbon Dots (CDs), the nanoparticles focus of the present work, had become to be a priority for the scientific research due to their non-toxicity and biocompatible properties [25]. Furthermore CDs also can be functionalized and conjugated with various compounds and therefore could be used in several analytical and bioanalytical applications [26]. A great number of methods for the CDs synthesis have proposed in last years. One of the most used method of synthesis is the microwave synthesis due to the fact that it is a rapid and simple method of CDs synthesis [27]. Different kind of precursors like carbohydrates, chitosan, citric acid (CA), amines and others can be used in CDs production [28,29]. Many CDs were microwave synthetized having as precursors CA combined with different amines [30–32]. Also the CDs can be doped to increase their quantum yield (Φ), to provide sensibility and selectivity as sensor to adjust their compositions and structures. Some works presented the CDs microwave synthesis from CA as carbon source and urea (UR) as nitrogen source [33–35].

Doping CDs with heteroatoms (e.g., oxygen, nitrogen, phosphor, boron or sulfur) allows adjusting their compositions and structures and is one way of tuning their electronic and optical properties [36]. Generally the quantum yield of the doped CDs is higher than the Φ of the non-doped CDs. Nitrogen doping CDs is a well established procedure [37,38]. More recently sulfur and nitrogen co-doped CDs prepared from various precursors have been reported [39–45]. The synergistic effect of the various dopants allows the synthesis of CDs with different characteristics and can have diverse applications [46]. The sulfur and nitrogen co-doped CDs commonly exhibit higher catalytic activity to the oxygen reduction reaction compared with the nitrogen doped CDs through a cooperative effect [36,47]. The co-doped sulfur atoms can help to enhance the effect of nitrogen doping through this cooperative effect. For the sulfur and nitrogen co-doped CDs, the sulfur atoms seems to be able to eliminate the O states and enhance the N states [48].

Two applications of CDs as NO nanoprobe were discussed [49,50]. In one of the works the ratiometric sensing of NO based in CDs through the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process was assessed [49]. More recently, a NO sensor based in CDs microwave synthesized from CA and EDA showed selective fluorescence sensing of NO and ONOO− in the presence of the main ROS/RNS. A greater sensitivity was obtained for the determination of ONOO− at pH 7 and 10 and a lower sensitivity for the determination of NO at pH 4 [50].

In this work a selective and sensitive sensor to NO detection and quantification at pH 7 based on microwave synthetized CDs prepared from CA, UR and sodium thiosulfate (ST) was developed. The fluorescence response of the synthetized CDs relatively to NO in the presence of other ROS/RNS and the NO quantification capability in standard and in fortified serum solutions were evaluated.

Citric Acid (CA), urea (UR), sodium thiosulfate (ST), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), hydrogen chloride (HCl), potassium superoxide (KO2), sodium hypochlorite 3.5% (NaClO), sodium nitrite (NaNO2), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), iron(II) sulfate (FeSO4·7H2O), iron(III) nitrate [Fe3(NO3)3·9H2O], magnesium chloride (MgCl2·6H2O), copper(II) sulfate (CuSO4·5H2O), sodium phosphate dibasic (Na2HPO4), monosodium phosphate (NaH2PO4), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) analytical grade reagents and Mili-Q water with a resistivity 18 MΩ cm at 25 °C were used in all the work.

Saturated NO solutions (1.9 mM) were prepared by bubbling argon for 15 min in water and follow bubbling NO another 15 min. ONOO− solutions were prepared in a refrigerated beaker under constant stirring by mixing 100 mL of NaNO2 600 mM, 100 mL of H2O2 600 mM in HCl 0.6 M and 100 mL of NaOH 3.6 M. The solution will turn yellow indicating the formation of ONOO−. The agitation is maintained until no O2 is formed. The hydroxyl (HO) was generated in situ by the Fenton reaction by addition of a 5 fold excess of H2O2 to the EDTA-Fe(II) complex formed with different concentrations of FeSO4. Serum fortified sample solutions were done respectively in water by rigorous dilution to the desired concentrations. The solutions of H2O2, KO2, NaClO, NaNO3, NaNO2 were prepared in water.

All the experiences were made at pH 7. The phosphate buffer 0.01 M (pH 7) used was prepared through weighting the required mass of NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4. Solutions of HCl and NaOH 0.1 M were also prepared in order to adjust the pH of the phosphate buffer solution and in order to evaluate the influence of pH in the CDs fluorescence.

The fluorescence sensing evaluations were made using a QE65000 charge-coupled detector, a 380 nm light emitting diode (LED), a sampling compartment (CUV-ALL-UV 4-way) and two 1.0 mm core diameter fiber optics (P1000-2-UV–VIS) from Ocean Optics. One of the fibers guides the light from the source to the sampling compartment and the other guide the emitted light to the detector. The reaction time profiles were obtained collecting the signal at the maximum emission wavelength, every 10 s with an integration time of 300 ms. A difference between the initial and the final fluorescence intensity measured after 10 min the addition of the ROS/RNS (intensity variation) were used in all the work.

The absorbance and fluorescence spectra were obtained in a standard 1 cm fluorescence quartz cell and collected respectively in a Jasco V-530 UV–Visible spectrophotometer and in a Jasco FP-6200 spectrofluorimeter. The absorption spectra were obtained in a wavelength range from 250 to 650 nm with a 2 nm interval, slit widths 2 nm and wavelength scan rate medium; the Fluorescence spectra were obtained in a wavelength range from 300 to 700 nm with a 1 nm interval, slit widths 5 nm, sensitivity response medium, response time fast and wavelength scan rate 1000 nm/min Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained by a Perkin Elmer FTIR Spectrum 400 in solid state with an attenuated total reflectance (ATR) accessory in a range of 4000 to 650 cm−1 with 16 accumulations and a resolution of 4 cm−1. The particle size, size distribution and shape of the synthetized CDs were evaluated by transmission electronic microscopy (TEM) in a FEI Company Tecnai G2 20 S-Twin electronic microscopic.

The more adequate CDs dilution in phosphate buffer 0.01 M was assessed by the evaluation of successive dilutions of CDs solution in order to obtain a higher fluorescence intensity. For the selected synthetized CDs, a 20 × dilution was chosen. The ROS/RNS addition, in the required concentration to the more adequate CDs dilution, was done in a cuvette with a Hamilton® syringe with permanent agitation while the fluorescence signal is continuously monitored for a period of 10 min.

The Φ of the synthetized CDs were calculated comparing the integrated photoluminescence intensities and the absorbance values of the synthesized CDs with the ones of the quinine sulfate.
Φ =
Φ R
×
G r a d
G r a
d R
×
η 2
η R 2

In the equation Φ is the fluorescence quantum yield, Grad is the gradient from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity vs. absorbance and η is the refractive index. The subscripts R refer to reference fluorophore, quinine sulfate of known quantum yield. The Φ of the quinine sulfate is Φ = 0.54 [38]. The η of the quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 and of nanocomposites water solutions used were η = 1.33.

It was previously observed that the CDs synthetized without ST show non-selectivity towards NO at pH 7 [35]. Consequently, in order to optimized the effect of ST in the increased sensitivity and selectivity of the response of the CDs towards NO a first set of formulations were prepared constituted by: m
CA = 1 g, m
UR = 1 g and m
ST of 0.5, 1, 2 and 3 g. The evaluation of the response of these CDs with the main ROS/RNS (NO, ONOO−, ClO−, HO, H2O2 and O2
−) was initially made at the concentrations 10, 50 and 100 μM (Fig. 1 and Fig. S1).

Higher selectivity to NO was now observed with the synthetized CDs with different quantities of ST and the highest was found for CDs synthesized with m
ST = 0.5 g and 3 g. CDs synthetized with m
ST = 1 g also respond to ONOO− and HO and the CDs synthetized with m
ST = 2 g also respond to ONOO−. Also CDs synthesized with a m
ST = 3 g show the greater sensitivity to NO (Fig. 1 and Fig. S1). Attending to these preliminary results the synthetized CDs with a m
CA = 1 g, m
UR = 1 g and m
ST = 3 g will be considered for the NO sensitivity and selectivity quantification at pH 7.

The characterization of the synthetized CDs with a m
CA = 1 g, m
UR = 1 g and m
ST = 3 g will be achieved by fluorescence, FTIR and TEM analysis. A sample of CDs synthesized without ST and with a m
ST = 0.5, 1 and 2 g was also analysed for comparative purposes. In Fig. 2 is presented the fluorescence excitation-emission matrices (EEM) of the synthetized CDs with a m
CA = 1 g, m
UR = 1 g and a m
ST = 3 g. (Fig. S2 show the EEM for CD without ST); the normalized absorbance, excitation and emission spectra (Fig. S3) and, for easier comparison, a table with the main fluorescence features (Table S1) of the all the synthetized CDs.

In comparison with the CDs without ST a decrease of the excitation, emission wavelength and of the Φ are evaluated with the CDs with ST. Relatively similar fluorescence characteristics are observed for the CDs with different quantities of ST. For all the CDs with different quantities of ST a similar excitation wavelength of 350 nm and a slightly decrease of the emission wavelength from 451 nm to 440 nm with the synthetized CDs with the increase of ST it's found. Relatively to the others synthetized CDs with ST the greater Φ it's evaluated with the synthetized CDs with an m
ST = 3 g. A similar excitation and emission and a slightly different absorbance spectra were found with the synthetized CDs without ST and with different quantities of ST.

The normalized fluorescence intensity variation with the pH and ionic strength of the CDs with a m
ST = 3 g is also shown in supplementary information (Fig. S4). The fluorescence intensity of the synthetized CDs with a m
CA = 1 g, m
UR = 1 g and a m
ST = 3 g is marked influenced by the pH with an increase from 2 to 6. A higher fluorescence intensity stability is observed above pH 6. The fluorescence intensity does not change with the ionic strength.

The FTIR spectra of the CDs without ST and with a m
ST = 3 g are presented in Fig. 3 . FTIR spectra of CDs with other contents of ST and of the three precursors are shown in Fig. S5 (Table S2).

Fig. 3 shows that different spectra were found with the CDs without and with ST. The main differences of the spectra are the strong broad band due to the OH/NH shifted from 3347 to 3191 cm−1, the existence of two new weakens sharp bands at 2068 and 2179 cm−1 respectively due to a CC and CN, the strong sharp band to the CN/CO shifted from 1568/1615 to 1579/1663 cm−1, the disappearance of a weaker sharp band at 1189 cm−1 due to CO and two new strong sharp band at 995 and 1112 cm−1 respectively due to a SO and CS. In comparison with the synthetized CDs with others quantities of ST a more similar spectrum was found with a m
ST = 2 g and the more different with a m
ST = 0.5 g.

The size and shape of the synthetized CDs with a mST = 3 g was evaluated by TEM analysis (Fig. 4 ). For these CDs a size of 1 and up to 4 nm is observed with a spherical or almost spherical shape. The statistical evaluation of the size distribution considering a sample of 100 nanoparticles shows:
x ¯
= 2.49 nm (σ = 0.57).

In order to assess the quantification capability of the NO by the CDs, the quenching effect, the recovery of NO in standard and fortified serum samples solutions and the selectivity were evaluated. In Fig. 5 is presented the fluorescence time profiles and the fluorescence quenching effect found by addition to the synthetized CDs with a m
CA = 1 g, m
UR = 1 g and m
ST = 3 g of NO at concentrations from 1 to 200 μM.

Similar fluorescence time profiles were obtained at different NO concentrations. A strong quenching was verified by addition of NO concentrations from 1 to 200 μM. It is possible to observe an initially linear variation with a deviation of linearity at higher NO concentrations. In supplementary information are presented the Stern-Volmer behaviour at two temperatures and the effect of KI in the fluorescence intensity. A linear behaviour described by the Stern-Volmer is verified by addition of NO concentration from 1 to 25 μM (Fig. S7). The evaluation of the Stern-Volmer behaviour at two temperatures and the calculation of the fraction of the accessible groups to iodide confirm that a dynamic quenching is not responsible for the decrease of fluorescence. Indeed, by increasing the temperature a decrease of this constant was observed. Besides, the addition of KI do not shows the expected variation in a dynamic quenching and do not allows the calculation of the fraction of the accessible groups to the iodide quenching. A static quenching mechanism is more probable due to the oxidation of S groups or even N groups due to the already described enhancement of N –states in CDs by a sulfur and nitrogen co-doping effect obtained by addition of sodium thiosulfate as precursor.

Attending the results of the NO addition from 1 to 200 μM to the CDs m
CA = 1 g, m
UR = 1 g and m
ST = 3 g (Fig. 5) the linearity of the ΔI (I – I0) in function of concentration was evaluated at two linear ranges from 1 to 25 μM and 1–50 μM. In supplementary information are presented the results found (Fig. S8). The more adequate linear concentration range is obtained in the concentration range from 1 to 25 μM. A linear decrease in the fluorescence of the synthetized CDs m
CA = 1 g, m
UR = 1 g and m
ST = 3 g was observed with a sensitivity of 16 and a detection limit of 0.3 μM.

In the more adequate linear range were obtained good quantification results with recoveries near 100% in the analysis of the standard and spiked serum samples at the levels of concentration evaluated (Table 1 ).

The interference study was done with the main ROS/RNS and ionic species by addition of a concentration of 25 μM to the synthetized CDs m
CA = 1 g, m
UR = 1 g and m
ST = 3 g (Fig. 6 ). A great selectivity was observed with these synthetized CDs. Only a small interference of Fe3+, Fe2+ and HO, maybe due to the excess of Fe2+ needed to the formation of HO, was evaluated.
Table 2 resumes the methods of synthesis, fluorescence characteristics and principal figures of merit of the already developed CDs based sensors for NO detection.

As already discussed in the introduction, up to the authors knowledge, only two papers report the use if CDs as NO sensors [49,50]. In one of them, CDs were synthesized from CA and EDA, and in the other, phenylenediamine-containing naphthalimide functionalized CDs synthetized from CA and EDA, were developed respectively as a quenching and a FRET ratiometric NO sensor. In the first one the quantification capability and in the second one the fluorescence imaging of NO were evaluated respectively at pH 4 and 7. Comparing the features of the CDs studied in this paper with other samples, a good selectivity, high sensitivity and a lower limit of detection are observed.

Sulfur and nitrogen co-doped CDs allow the NO quantification with great sensitivity and selectivity. A strong fluorescence quenching effect was verified by addition of NO to sulfur and nitrogen co-doped synthetized CDs with a m
CA = 1 g, m
UR = 1 g and a m
ST = 3 g. The great increase in the NO sensitivity and selectivity detection is due to the effect of sodium thiosulfate by sulfur co-doping nitrogen CDs that alters the intrinsic characteristics of the nitrogen doping effect.

Good quantification results were assessed in standard solutions and in spiked serum samples. Only the Fe2+ and Fe3+ ionic specie causes a small interference in the NO quantification. The evaluated analytical methodology for NO quantification shows a linear response in a range from 1 to 25 μM with a sensitivity of 16 μM−1 and a detection limit of 0.3 μM.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: A PhD grant SFRH/BD/81074/2011 to Eliana Simões and financial support, project PTDC/QEQQAN/5955/2014, from Fundação para a Ciência e a Tecnologia (FCT, Lisbon) are acknowledged. The use of FTIR from UCQFarma, Pharmacy Faculty of the University of Coimbra and the TEM analyses from Nanomaterials and Micromanufacturing group of Centre for Mechanical Engineering of the University of Coimbra (CEMUC) are also acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2017.01.007.

Carbon dots-based fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective detection of iodide
Highly luminescent carbon nanodots by microwave-assisted pyrolysis
Carbon dots for copper detection with down and upconversion fluorescent properties as excitation sources
A biocompatible fluorescent ink based on water-soluble luminescent carbon nanodots
Green and simple route toward boron doped carbon dots with significantly enhanced non-linear optical properties

Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots with strong blue luminescence

A fluorescent ratiometric nanosensor for detecting NO in aqueous media and imaging exogenous and endogenous NO in live cells
